items.title: The top of the K is cracking: college-educated millennials joining the bust
items.idea: Deluard (StoneX Global Macro) has been bullish on the US consumer every time he's appeared — until now. April saw a massive drop in personal savings, college-educated millennial incomes collapsed, tax returns are down, software companies are laying off left and right, home equity loans are picking up, and credit delinquencies across cards, autos, and student loans are ALL at record highs simultaneously. His thesis: we have been in 'permanent stimulus' since COVID, but the net keeps narrowing — from universal CARES payments to One Big Beautiful Bill (working class) to now just data center buildout + tariff refunds + a coming military blowout. Even the 'K' economy's upper tier is now cracking.
items.angle: This is the most important bear signal I've seen on US consumer: when even the college-educated tier starts cracking, the AI-fuelled market narrative hits a wall. Key trade implication: beware the late-cycle IPO pump (SpaceX, Anthropic) that props things until summer, then look for a top.
items.tweetA: Deluard has been bullish on the US consumer every single time he's appeared — until today. April: massive savings drop. College-educated millennial incomes collapsed. Home equity loans picking up. Credit delinquencies at ALL-TIME highs across cards, autos, and student loans at once. This is the 'K' economy — and now the top of the K is cracking.
items.tweetB: The bull thesis is: AI IPOs (SpaceX, Anthropic) keep the market running until summer. The bear thesis is: you can't manufacture a recovery when the only stimulus left is data center buildout. Deluard says tariff refunds are already larger than tariff collections. Permanent stimulus is narrowing to nothing.

items.title: Inflation is cocaine — and we're entering the hangover phase
items.idea: Deluard's framework: inflation phase 1 is euphoric (revenue grows, margins look great because costs are booked at historical values, multiples expand). Phase 2 is when the hangover hits — people realize 'it wasn't growth, it was inflation.' He says we've 'underreported inflation for a long time and some of the growth is really just the inflation illusion.' We are a stagflationary environment, 'but no one's going to admit it.' Historical parallel: late 1960s inflation → margins first rose, then collapsed when phase 2 hit.
items.angle: This framework explains why earnings look solid even as the consumer cracks: nominal revenue growth masks real volume decline. If Deluard is right, the earnings season disappointment is coming. Watch for margin compression even as revenues stay nominally elevated.
items.tweetA: Inflation is cocaine. First hit: revenue surges, margins expand, multiples stretch. Everyone feels great. Second phase: you realize it wasn't growth — it was price. That's when the hangover hits. Deluard says we've just entered phase 2. The market hasn't noticed yet.
items.tweetB: We've been 'underreporting inflation for a long time' and 'some of the growth is really just the inflation illusion.' Not my words — those are from a historically US-consumer-bullish strategist who just turned bear. If he's right, real EPS is a lot lower than the tape suggests.

items.title: Alphabet's CapEx math is staggering: FCF $73B → $16B; debt $22.5B → $90.5B in 12 months
items.idea: Google generated ~$73B of free cash flow in both 2024 and 2025. They are now raising $80B in equity (only second equity raise since the 2004 IPO). Why? Because FCF is expected to plunge to $21B in 2026 and $16B in 2027, while CapEx goes from $91.4B (2025) to $186B (2026E) to ~$250B (2027E). Long-term debt exploded from $22.5B on 3/31/25 to $90.5B on 3/31/26 — a 4x increase in one year. Chris Wood at Jefferies shows this is the largest corporate CapEx commitment since WWII. 'Asset light no longer.' HP Enterprise also reported a blowout quarter.
items.angle: The market treats Alphabet CapEx as a positive signal. But FCF going from $73B to $16B while debt quadruples in 12 months is not a growth story — it's a transformation into a capital-intensive infrastructure company with tech multiples. At what point does the market reprice $GOOGL like a utility?
items.tweetA: Google's FCF: $73B in 2025. Expected FCF: $16B in 2027. Long-term debt: $22.5B in March 2025. Now: $90.5B — a 4x increase in 12 months. They're raising $80B in equity (only their second share sale since 2004). 'Asset light' was the entire valuation premise. It no longer applies. $GOOGL
items.tweetB: Google needs to raise $80B in equity AND quadruple its debt load because CapEx is going from $91B to $250B in two years. Meanwhile FCF collapses from $73B to $16B. This is what 'winning the AI race' looks like from inside a P&L. The question is: what multiple should you pay for a hyperscaler that's burning through its own business model? $GOOGL

charts.desc: Chris Wood (Jefferies) post-WWII CapEx chart showing current datacenter buildout is largest corporate investment wave in modern history. CHART WORTH SAVING

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: AI is the new inflation engine — the real price pressure is from server racks, not oil tankers
items.idea: While markets fixate on Iran/Hormuz, the more durable inflation story is unfolding inside data centers. The AI buildout is reshaping factory activity, capital spending, supply chains, pricing power, and potentially central bank policy. Anthropic's IPO values it at ~$1T; SpaceX at ~$1.75T. Alphabet raising $80B in equity including private placement with Berkshire Hathaway. This is not routine fundraising — it's large enough to alter benchmark composition, shift index weightings, and further concentrate market leadership into a narrow AI group.
items.angle: Two inflation signals in one: (1) input cost inflation from AI buildout hitting supply chains and factory costs; (2) equity supply inflation from an unprecedented IPO pipeline. Both compress returns. The irony: every dollar of AI CapEx is inflationary, yet the AI stocks are the ones the market uses to 'hedge' inflation.
items.tweetA: Everyone's watching Hormuz. The bigger inflation story is happening in server farms. AI buildout is reshaping supply chains, factory activity, and now central bank calculus. Anthropic at $1T, SpaceX at $1.75T, Google raising $80B equity. This isn't corporate finance — it's a tectonic event for index weights. $QQQ $SPY
items.tweetB: Debt markets absorb borrowing. Equity markets absorb dilution. Right now we have Anthropic (~$1T), SpaceX (~$1.75T), and Alphabet ($80B equity raise) all coming at once. The question isn't whether AI is worth it — the question is whether there's enough demand to absorb all this supply at these prices.

items.title: Anthropic: $45B ARR, 130% quarterly growth, first operating profit — and 'tokenmaxxing' is the killer app
items.idea: Anthropic has blown past OpenAI to ~$45B annualized revenue, growing at ~130% per quarter, and is about to turn its first operating profit. Valuation at IPO: ~$1T = 20x annualized revenue. The killer app: coding agents (Claude Code). Companies are 'tokenmaxxing' — one company reportedly spent $500M on Claude Code alone (= 1% of Anthropic's annualized revenue). But Noah's core question: what is all this code actually producing? App Store submissions are way up. But new user-facing features? Not obviously visible.
items.angle: The $500M spent by one company on Claude Code = 1% of Anthropic's entire ARR. If a handful of large enterprises account for a disproportionate share of revenue, the IPO multiple depends entirely on them not finding a cheaper substitute. Concentration risk at $1T valuation.
items.tweetA: Anthropic is growing at 130% per quarter. 20x revenue = 'pretty conservative.' One company spent $500M on Claude Code — that's 1% of Anthropic's entire annual revenue from a single customer. This is not a distributed consumer business. It's a B2B enterprise story with extreme concentration. That's the risk the IPO price doesn't reflect.
items.tweetB: Everyone in the AI world is 'tokenmaxxing' — spending as much as possible on coding agents without asking what they're building. Meta had a leaderboard for who consumed the most tokens. A company told its employees to 'spend their salary in tokens.' If you can't answer 'what is the output,' that's not a business — it's a cargo cult.

items.title: Software saturation question: how much more do we really need?
items.idea: Noah's deeper concern beneath the Anthropic IPO: we may be approaching software saturation. Anthropic found its killer app in coding agents, but the output is unclear. More App Store submissions, not more useful apps. Companies are incentivizing employees to use as many tokens as possible, but the value created per token may be declining.
items.angle: Software saturation is an underpriced risk in the AI thesis. If token consumption can't translate to measurable productivity gain, the $1T Anthropic valuation = betting the second derivative of software consumption is infinite. History says it isn't.
items.tweetA: The internet needed e-commerce. Computers needed spreadsheets. What does the age of coding agents need? That's the question under the Anthropic IPO. Everyone's consuming tokens. Nobody can answer what they're building. 'Tokenmaxxing' is a consumption metric, not a production metric.
items.tweetB: One company spent half a billion dollars on Claude Code. When asked what their employees were building with it, there was no clear answer. At 130% quarterly growth and $1T valuation, Anthropic is pricing in the assumption that the answer becomes obvious soon. What if it doesn't?

items.title: VC shift to atoms: 'Software is dead for VC returns' — the 50-year blip thesis
items.idea: Silicon Valley investor Marvin Liao: AI has commoditized software, making pure SaaS 'DOA' for venture returns. The 'Atom Moat' thesis: value now accrues to regulated, capex-intensive, physical-world businesses. Steve Yegge: 'If you have a strictly online or SaaS presence with no atoms in your product, you are pretty screwed if you don't pivot.' BuccoCapital: 'For 50 years we treated asset-light business supremacy as a permanent economic law. AI commoditizing it would just be reverting to the historical mean.' Liao moved to defense tech in 2023 on instinct.
items.angle: This is a generational VC thesis shift: energy, defense, manufacturing, logistics, physical infrastructure get institutional capital flows that software once absorbed. The 'physics over software' trade is not just rhetoric — it's where the smart GP money is moving.
items.tweetA: For 50 years, asset-light software businesses were treated as a permanent economic law. AI is making software a commodity. If that's true, we're not in a revolution — we're reverting to the historical mean where value accrued to atoms, infrastructure, and energy. A 50-year anomaly correcting back. What does your portfolio look like in that world?
items.tweetB: Silicon Valley GPs who've been doing software for 30 years are quietly pivoting to defense tech, reindustrialization, and physical infrastructure. Not because they love atoms — because AI just made their entire asset class a commodity. 'Physics over software' isn't a meme. It's where the smart money moved two years ago.

items.title: S&P +16% in 2 months: since WWII this has only preceded a recession exit or a crash
items.idea: Deutsche Bank (via Bond Beat): the S&P 500 is up +16% in two months — a pace that since WWII has only been seen coming out of a recession (GFC, COVID-19) or immediately before a major crash (Black Monday 1987). Rosenberg notes 9 consecutive weekly gains — only occurred 10 times since 1950, 'generally reserved for early-cycle when Fed is easing and oil is falling.' Now during an energy shock with stretched valuations in a mature cycle. 5yr yield at resistance (4.10%), momentum deeply overbought.
items.angle: The 1987 parallel is the one to sit with: massive rally into a crash, NOT a recession start. The distribution is bimodal: either coming out of a recession not yet declared, or on borrowed time. Market is pricing the former; bonds are pricing neither.
items.tweetA: S&P up 16% in two months. Deutsche Bank: since WWII, this speed of two-month gain only happened coming out of a recession OR right before Black Monday 1987. We're not in a recession (officially). So either the data is wrong, or we're in the second category. The bond market, sitting at 4.43% on the 10yr, is not agreeing with the equity party.
items.tweetB: Nine consecutive weekly gains for the S&P — only happened ten times since 1950. Every prior instance: Fed was easing AND oil was falling. Today: energy shock, mature cycle, stretched valuations. Rosenberg says this is early-cycle behavior happening in a late-cycle world. Something is about to give. Either the data or the tape.

charts.desc: DB chart: S&P 2-month Rate of Change +16% vs historical distribution. Bond Beat 5yr yield daily momentum overbought at 4.10% resistance. CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Manufacturing PMI 4-year high — but it's all stockpiling driven by Hormuz/tariff fears, not organic demand
items.idea: May manufacturing PMI hit a 4-year high. But Chris Williamson warns: production buoyed by stock building as companies worry over Hormuz closure + tariff fears. Supply chain delays highest since August 2022. Producer costs spiking — 'worrying signal that broader economy inflation has further to rise.' Employment rising less than production ('automation and efficiencies'). 'Growth will cool once this stock build has run its course.'
items.angle: Beware the PMI headline: this looks like a demand signal but it's a fear signal. Companies are building inventory because they're scared. Once the stockpile is in place, demand collapses. This is a spring-loaded stagflation setup.
items.tweetA: Manufacturing PMI just hit a 4-year high. Sounds bullish. But the entire surge is stockpiling — companies panic-buying ahead of Hormuz closure and tariff fears. Supply chain delays: highest since August 2022. Once the inventory build runs its course, production cools and costs stay hot. That's the definition of stagflation.
items.tweetB: Supply chain delays highest since August 2022. Producer costs spiking. Employment barely rising even as production surges ('automation'). The manufacturing boom that everyone's celebrating is front-loaded fear, not actual demand. When the stock build unwinds, the stagflation signal underneath becomes visible.

items.title: Saylor sells BTC for the first time since 2022 — the balance sheet math explains why this matters
items.idea: Strategy disclosed selling 32 BTC at $77,135 average = $2.5M. First sale since December 2022. Strategy carries ~$13.5B in total preferred equity obligations with $1.2-1.4B annual dividend burden, against cash of just $871M = less than 9 months coverage without new STRC issuance. Saylor has transformed from 'passive buyer who never sells' to 'active fund manager buying and selling.' BTC fell to $69,400 (-6% on week), Strategy -6% on Monday. Strategy raised $128.3M selling common shares the same week — dwarfing the BTC sale 50x. Polymarket market resolving as NO despite disclosure.
items.angle: The signal isn't $2.5M of BTC sales — it's that the psychological contract with the market is broken. Saylor's brand was 'never sell.' Now that it's 'sell when needed,' every downleg in BTC becomes a question of whether Strategy needs to sell more. That's a new overhang.
items.tweetA: Strategy sold 32 BTC for $2.5M. The number is irrelevant. What changed: Saylor went from the world's most famous 'never sell' hodler to an active BTC manager with $1.2-1.4B of annual preferred dividend obligations and only $871M cash. Less than 9 months of runway. Every BTC dip is now 'will he sell again?' $MSTR $BTC
items.tweetB: Strategy raised $128M selling common shares the same week they sold $2.5M of BTC. The irony: they're using their own stock — priced on BTC enthusiasm — to fund the preferred dividends on the product of that same enthusiasm. It works until BTC stops going up. $MSTR

items.title: BTC ETF -$484M, Fear & Greed 22, Mt Gox $739M moved — three separate overhangs converge
items.idea: Three forces hit BTC simultaneously: (1) Strategy sells first BTC since 2022; (2) BTC ETFs see -$484M in outflows (ETH ETFs: -$44M); (3) Mt Gox moved $739M of BTC on-chain. Fear & Greed: 22 (extreme fear). BTC at $69,400, down 6% on week. Also: Vitalik rethinks DeFi, Grayscale HYPE ETF 'likely imminent,' Bitwise bought $20M HYPE in single day, OpenSea teases HYPE perps, Binance opens US stocks to non-US users.
items.angle: Mt Gox supply + ETF outflows + Strategy-as-seller = triple overhang at once. Meanwhile HYPE ecosystem is the one bright spot — Grayscale imminent + Bitwise $20M single-day buy = conviction bet.
items.tweetA: Three BTC overhangs converged at once: Strategy sells for first time since 2022. ETF outflows: $484M. Mt Gox moves $739M on-chain. Fear & Greed hits 22. BTC at $69.4k. The 'institutional adoption' narrative requires institutions to buy more than they sell. Right now they're selling. $BTC
items.tweetB: Buried in the BTC carnage: Bitwise bought $20M of HYPE in a single day. Grayscale HYPE ETF described as 'likely imminent.' OpenSea teasing HYPE perps. HYPE hit $75 for the first time before pulling back. When BTC bleeds, find what's getting institutional conviction anyway. $HYPE

items.title: 50+ banks adding crypto rails via SWIFT; Schwab spots crypto 2027; TON → GRAM rebrands +13%
items.idea: Multiple structural developments: 50+ banks set to add crypto rails via SWIFT. Schwab targets 2027 for spot crypto trading. Pavel rebrands Toncoin as GRAM, briefly +13%. Strive proposes $4.2B BTC ATM raise. Bitmine bought 26.5K ETH = 4.5% of total supply. SOL has fallen for 8 months straight. CME announced 24/7 crypto futures — stock fell anyway.
items.angle: SWIFT + 50 banks is the slow-moving enormous story. Bitmine holding 4.5% of ETH supply is significant concentration not widely discussed. SOL -8 months straight = real ecosystem fatigue.
items.tweetA: 50+ banks are adding crypto rails via SWIFT. Schwab is building spot crypto trading for 2027. CME just announced 24/7 crypto futures. None of this is reflected in a Fear & Greed of 22. Infrastructure is being built while everyone panics about price. $BTC $ETH
items.tweetB: Bitmine holds 4.5% of all circulating ETH supply. That's 26.5K ETH — a level of concentration that doesn't get mentioned. SOL has fallen for 8 consecutive months. TON just renamed to GRAM. The altcoin landscape is being quietly reorganized while attention is on BTC/Saylor. $ETH $SOL

items.title: Pope Leo XIV's AI encyclical 'Magnifica humanitas': the Vatican as neutral AI arbiter while tech/govts fail
items.idea: On May 25, 2026, Pope Leo XIV released 'Magnifica humanitas' — the first papal encyclical on AI. He chose his name after Leo XIII, whose 'Rerum novarum' addressed the Industrial Revolution. Chris Olah of Anthropic sat alongside the Pope at the ceremony. The encyclical criticizes companies like Anthropic while Anthropic apparently provided technical background. The secular world is watching because the Pope is seen as impartial — he doesn't profit from AI doing better or worse.
items.angle: This is a governance signal, not a theological one. When the world looks to the Vatican for AI regulation, it signals existing governance frameworks have lost credibility as neutral arbiters. The encyclical legitimizes international, values-based AI governance that cuts across national interests.
items.tweetA: The Pope just released the first-ever AI encyclical. Chris Olah of Anthropic sat next to him at the ceremony. Why does Silicon Valley care what the Pope thinks about AI? Because the only people who DON'T financially or politically benefit from AI going well are... the Vatican. The world is searching for a neutral arbiter and has run out of options.
items.tweetB: Pope Leo XIV chose his name after Leo XIII — who wrote Rerum novarum during the Industrial Revolution. He's explicitly comparing AI to industrialization. The encyclical criticizes the companies that helped write it. That's not hypocrisy — that's the Church doing exactly what the Church does. And Silicon Valley is paying attention. That alone is the story.

items.title: 4 AI supercycle bottlenecks: compute, memory, power — and now photonics
items.idea: AI Supremacy identifies 4 bottlenecks: compute, memory (HBM chip shortage), power, and now photonics. Google raising $80B including $10B private placement from Berkshire Hathaway. Meta may become a 'glorified Neo Cloud as a side gig.' SpaceX selling compute to Anthropic pre-IPO 'just to make ends meet.' Supply constraint moving from compute to interconnect (photonics) as next binding limit.
items.angle: Photonics as the 4th bottleneck is the least-priced insight here. If photonic interconnects are limiting AI scaling, that's a direct signal for optical networking stocks. Berkshire buying $10B of Google equity (not debt) = Buffett endorsing hyperscaler capex returns.
items.tweetA: The AI supercycle has 4 bottlenecks: compute, memory (HBM shortage), power, and now photonics. We've largely priced the first three. Photonic interconnects as a binding constraint at scale? That's the next supply chain story. And Berkshire Hathaway just put $10B into Google equity. Buffett is comfortable with hyperscaler capex returns. $GOOGL $NVDA
items.tweetB: SpaceX is selling compute to Anthropic pre-IPO just to fund operations. Meta may become a Neo Cloud as a side gig. The AI arms race is so capital-intensive that some of the world's most valuable companies are becoming secondary players in their own narrative. This is what a genuine supercycle looks like from inside.

items.title: 220+ fallen unicorns; half of America's 857 unicorns haven't raised in 3 years — AI eating its own ecosystem
items.idea: Nearly half of America's 857 unicorn startups haven't raised fresh funding in 3 years (PitchBook). 220+ are now 'fallen unicorns.' Mechanism: $250B+ funneled into OpenAI and Anthropic, resetting valuations for entire startup classes. AI-native post-2023 companies are being funded; pre-GPT generation is being abandoned.
items.angle: 220 fallen unicorns is a venture-backed debt/equity overhang that will surface as write-downs at LPs (pension funds, endowments, sovereign wealth). This feeds through to asset allocators in 12-24 months when fund marks can't be sustained.
items.tweetA: 220+ unicorns are now 'fallen unicorns.' Half of America's 857 unicorn startups haven't raised in 3 years. AI didn't just disrupt industries — it disrupted the venture ecosystem itself. $250B poured into OpenAI and Anthropic reset what 'fundable' means. There's a write-down wave coming at the LP level. Pension funds haven't seen it yet.
items.tweetB: Pre-2023 startups are being starved of capital because VCs are funding AI-native companies instead. The creative destruction is happening inside venture, not just in the markets they invest in. The question: what happens to 220 unicorns that can't raise, can't exit, and can't wind down quietly?

items.title: Pearl Research: Proof-of-Useful-Work — mine crypto by doing actual AI matrix multiplication
items.idea: Pearl Research replaces SHA-256 hashing with matrix multiplications inside AI training and inference. The claim: the work that mines the coin is identical to work a GPU was already doing for an AI model. Token (PRL) value designed to track global AI compute demand. Partnered with Together AI for inference endpoint (Gemma-4-31B) priced 25%+ below market. Also tracking: TIG (algorithms as mineable asset) and Prism (token as LP position).
items.angle: Proof-of-Useful-Work is the most intellectually interesting crypto primitive in years. If it works, it solves the 'Bitcoin wastes energy' criticism by making energy productive. If AI compute demand compounds, PRL becomes a direct derivative on AI infrastructure growth without equity dilution risk.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin burns energy on a calculation that produces nothing useful. Pearl Research replaces it with the matrix multiplications inside actual AI training. Mining the token = running the model. If AI compute demand compounds, the token compounds with it. This is the most interesting crypto primitive in years. Not hype — novel mechanism.
items.tweetB: The logic: SHA-256 is arbitrary work. Matrix multiplication is the work that already dominates global compute spend. What if you made that the mining operation? Pearl: mine PRL by doing real AI inference. Token value tracks AI compute demand. Partner: Together AI. I'm watching this.

items.title: Global liquidity $193.29T: edging higher but on narrow drivers — EM turning, risk appetite peaking
items.idea: Global liquidity edged higher to $193.29T. But: narrowly driven — currency headwinds and ongoing CB tightening restricting broader expansion. Investor risk appetite showing signs of peaking. EM exposure already heading lower — early signal of defensive rotation. Narrow liquidity drivers + peaking risk appetite + EM outflows suggests the tide is close to turning.
items.angle: Howell's liquidity framework has been the best macro leading indicator this cycle. EM exposure heading lower while developed market indices still rally = bifurcation that historically precedes broader risk-off. Watch EM credit and currency as the canary.
items.tweetA: Global liquidity just hit $193.29T — but Howell flags it's all narrow drivers. EM exposure is already falling. Risk appetite showing signs of peaking. Currency headwinds. CB tightening restricting expansion. The tide isn't rising broadly — it's a narrowing stream. That usually precedes a turn. $EEM
items.tweetB: Liquidity at a record high but EM turning lower first. That's the classic sequence before a broader risk-off: EM cracks first, then spreads, then developed markets notice. Howell's data is flashing this right now at $193.29T.

items.title: June 2 market snapshot: crude above $90, gold +1%, BTC -3%, S&P +20% from March low — breadth razor narrow
items.idea: Market conditions June 2: crude -1.5% but above $90/bbl; 10yr -5bps to 4.43%; gold +1%+; Bitcoin -3% below $70k; S&P futures -0.2%. S&P is up ~20% from late-March low but breadth is razor narrow — gains concentrated in Technology. Eurozone May CPI: 3.2% y/y (in-line), core 2.5% vs 2.4% expected. Europe STOXX 600 +0.7% led by Italy/Germany. Software names 'grabbing baton' from AI hardware.
items.angle: Eurozone core CPI miss (2.5 vs 2.4) combined with oil above $90 keeps ECB trapped. S&P 500 breadth divergence most important technical reality: 20% rally with narrow breadth resolves via broadening OR rollover.
items.tweetA: June 2 setup: Oil still above $90 despite -1.5% today. Gold +1%. BTC -3% below $70k. Eurozone core CPI: 2.5% vs 2.4% expected. The inflation signals are still hot across every asset class simultaneously — except the S&P 500, which is up 20% from the March low on razor-thin breadth. One of these things is lying.
items.tweetB: S&P +20% from March but almost entirely in Technology. Europe STOXX +0.7% today, led by Italy and Germany. Software names trying to 'grab the baton' from AI hardware. Either breadth broadens from here and the rally generalizes — or the narrow leaders roll over and the 20% becomes a 10%. No middle ground.

items.title: June 1 earnings: MongoDB +20% (AI workloads), Cadence +10.5%, Zoom + on Anthropic IPO stake, Cinemark record
items.idea: June 1 notable moves: MongoDB +20.4% on AI-related database workloads. Cadence Design Systems +10.5% on AI chip design acceleration. Zoom rose because Anthropic's IPO filing increases value of Zoom's equity stake. Salesforce, ServiceNow, Workday all rising on enterprise AI pivot. HP Inc: new Nvidia-powered PC. IBM: Barclays Overweight $350 PT. MGM: take-private proposal. Cinemark: record May box office AND record F&B spending per customer. CME fell despite 24/7 crypto futures. Constellation Energy declined after 11M share secondary.
items.angle: Zoom as an Anthropic proxy is new — ZOOM goes up on AI IPO headlines without doing anything operationally. Cinemark record F&B per customer = premium experience consumers still spending even as mass-market consumer cracks.
items.tweetA: Zoom went up because Anthropic filed for an IPO. Zoom owns Anthropic equity. The market is creating Anthropic proxies everywhere. When the actual IPO lands, who actually benefits vs who already priced it in? The tail is wagging the dog. $ZM
items.tweetB: Cinemark: record May domestic box office. Record food-and-beverage spend per customer. Movie theaters were supposed to be dead. Deluard says upper-income consumers are cracking. But they're spending MORE per person when they DO show up. Premium experiences surviving the consumer squeeze — base mass-market is the actual risk. $CNK

charts.desc: Daily percent change & volume chart for June 1 movers vs 3-month standard deviation

items.title: CME fell despite 24/7 crypto futures; Chipotle testing 'happy hour' — crowded trades and brand cracks
items.idea: CME Group announced 24/7 cryptocurrency futures and options — stock fell. Constellation Energy declined after 11M share secondary below prior close. Chipotle fell after discussing discounted 'happy hour' testing. Common theme: 'good news, stock down' signals crowded trades already fully priced. Chipotle testing discounts = brand that built premium positioning on never discounting is now responding to consumer pressure.
items.angle: CME 24/7 crypto = sell the news. Constellation = nuclear hype fully priced. Chipotle happy hour = the consumer squeeze is real enough to reach premium fast casual. Three separate 'crowded trade' signals in one day.
items.tweetA: CME announced 24/7 crypto futures. Stock fell. Constellation priced a secondary below market. Stock fell. 'Good news, stock down' is the clearest signal of a crowded trade. The market was already long both. When confirmation arrives, there's no one left to buy. $CME $CEG
items.tweetB: Chipotle is testing discounted 'happy hour.' Chipotle. The chain that built its entire brand on never running promotions. When Chipotle starts discounting, it tells you the consumer squeeze is real enough to reach premium fast casual. Not a macro data point — a behavioral inflection. $CMG

items.title: Bond-Bullion Barbell works; Rosenberg: 'the new math for AI simply doesn't work'
items.idea: Rosenberg's bond-bullion barbell had a rare positive day. His framing: 9 straight weekly S&P gains during 'a huge energy shock' is 'nothing short of remarkable' — behavior 'reserved for early-cycle when Fed is easing and oil is falling.' Last comparable run: December 12, 1985. Risk premia have 'evaporated at precisely the wrong point in the cycle.' Core claim: 'The new math for AI simply doesn't work, whether today's investor base realizes it or not.'
items.angle: Rosenberg has been wrong on equities for a year but his framework is internally consistent. Deluard and Rosenberg independently landing on the same structure from different directions. The 'risk premia evaporate in mature phase' argument is convergent across multiple analysts.
items.tweetA: The S&P just had its 9th straight weekly gain — during an energy shock, in a mature cycle, with evaporated risk premia. Rosenberg: 'nothing short of remarkable.' The last comparable run was December 1985. You know what happened between 1985 and 1987? The market doubled. Then Black Monday. The setup is being replicated — people just disagree about which part of the sequence we're in.
items.tweetB: Rosenberg: 'The new math for AI simply doesn't work, whether today's investor base realizes it or not.' He's been saying this for 18 months. The market has been wrong. But the sentence that should keep you up: risk premia have 'evaporated' at exactly the point in the cycle when they historically re-emerge. $SPY $TLT $GLD

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Anthropic ~$1T IPO + SpaceX ~$1.75T: equity market-cap events that will alter index weights
items.idea: Anthropic filed confidentially for IPO, likely September-October. SpaceX expected later this month. Combined ~$2.75T of new public market cap in months. If both enter major indices, passive funds must buy proportionally — altering benchmark composition and forcing capital concentration. Alphabet's $80B equity raise includes Berkshire Hathaway private placement — equity dilution creates persistent supply pressure, different from debt.
items.angle: Index inclusion mechanics: if Anthropic at $1T and SpaceX at $1.75T enter S&P 500 or Nasdaq-100, passive funds sell existing members to buy newcomers. Existing AI stocks in the index could paradoxically fall on the announcement of an AI company going public.
items.tweetA: Anthropic $1T + SpaceX $1.75T coming to public markets in the next few months. If either enters the S&P 500, passive funds have to buy — and they fund that by selling existing members. The AI names already in the index could FALL on the announcement of an AI company going public. Index mechanics are weird. $SPY $QQQ
items.tweetB: Alphabet raising $80B in equity — only its second share sale since 2004. The first was followed by two decades of buybacks. Now they're going the other direction. Index rebalancing, forced dilution, shrinking FCF. $GOOGL is morphing from tech company to infrastructure utility in real time.

items.title: Microcap data: $1k (1928) → $870M micro cap vs $8.7M large cap; 87% of 1000%+ gainers were microcaps
items.idea: Historical compounding data since 1928: large caps = $8.7M; small caps = $430M; micro caps = $870M; high-quality micro caps = $1.26B from $1,000. 82% of stocks that rose 1,000%+ (2012-2022) were already profitable when they made the move. 87% of global stocks rising 1,000%+ last decade were microcaps. Key institutional blind spot: large funds can't buy microcaps without moving the price 20%+ before they hold a meaningful position. Buffett: 'If I only invested $1M I could earn 50% a year.'
items.angle: The microcap institutional blind spot is structural and compounds with AI: AI research agents will partially democratize microcap due diligence over time, making the blind spot smaller. The window of pure human research advantage is narrowing.
items.tweetA: $1,000 in large caps in 1928 = $8.7M today. Same $1,000 in high-quality microcaps = $1.26 BILLION. The difference: institutions are too big to buy them. Buffett said he could earn 50% a year if he only managed $1M. That edge still exists. The question is whether AI research tools are about to erode it.
items.tweetB: 82% of stocks that rose 1,000%+ from 2012-2022 were already profitable when they made the move. 87% were microcaps. Find the profitable small company before it's discovered, not after. The market for big caps is efficiently priced. The market for sub-$300M companies is not — because the buyers are too big to participate.

items.title: We are paying more in tariff refunds than we are collecting in tariffs
items.idea: Buried in Deluard's analysis: tariff refunds are now exceeding tariff collections. The fiscal math that was supposed to fund the One Big Beautiful Bill tax cuts isn't materializing. The 'permanent stimulus' leaks: data center buildout (hyperscaler GDP), tariff refunds (net Treasury drain), upcoming military blowout budget. None of these reach the median consumer who is hitting record credit delinquencies.
items.angle: If tariff refunds > tariff collections, the entire revenue-offset narrative for tax cuts collapses. Deficit expands. Bond vigilantes already at 4.43% on 10yr have one more reason to push higher. This is a fiscal math detail that the mainstream hasn't priced.
items.tweetA: Deluard: 'We are paying more in tariff refunds than we are collecting in tariffs.' The fiscal math that was supposed to fund the tax cuts isn't working. If the tariff revenue isn't there, the deficit expands. The bond vigilantes who are already at 4.43% on the 10yr have one more reason to push higher. $TLT
items.tweetB: The tariff policy is running a net deficit: more refunds than collections. The data center boom contributes to GDP but doesn't employ median workers. The military budget blowout is coming later this year. None of these reach the consumer hitting record credit delinquencies. 'Permanent stimulus' is real — it's just permanently missing the people who need it.

items.title: IPO frenzy (SpaceX, Anthropic) keeps market propped until summer — then Deluard expects a top
items.idea: Despite his new bearish lean, Deluard thinks the speculative frenzy around upcoming IPOs (SpaceX, Anthropic) is 'probably going to keep the market going until the summer.' Market top expected summer or fall. Implication: 2-4 months of IPO-driven momentum before macro reality reasserts itself. Not a crash call — more a grinding reassessment.
items.angle: IPO windows as timing mechanisms are underappreciated. The SpaceX IPO (expected 'later this month') and Anthropic (September/October) = the market peak signal, not the valuation catalyst. When the last IPO price is set, the last buyer is found.
items.tweetA: Deluard: the IPO frenzy around SpaceX and Anthropic will keep the market elevated through summer. After that — top. It's a useful framework: the IPO window IS the late-cycle blowoff signal. Every major cycle ends with a flurry of hot offerings at peak valuations. We're in that window right now.
items.tweetB: Peak cycles don't end with a crash — they end with an IPO. AOL Time Warner. Pets.com. WeWork (almost). Now: Anthropic at $1T, SpaceX at $1.75T, both in the same summer. Deluard sees a top this fall. I keep coming back to the question: who's the marginal buyer after the IPOs close?
